Denary: We convert the binary result back into Denary before showing it to the user.
Bit: A single Bit can store only a 0 or a 1.
Byte: Storing one ASCII character takes exactly one Byte of memory.
Nibble: Each hexadecimal digit maps neatly onto one Nibble of four bits.
ASCII: The letter A is stored as 65 in the ASCII table.
Two's complement: Using Two's complement, the processor can subtract by simply adding a negative number.
Overflow: Adding two large integers in an 8-bit register can cause an Overflow.
Bitmap image: Enlarging a Bitmap image too far makes the individual pixels visible.
Resolution: A photo at a higher Resolution contains more pixels and shows finer detail.
Sample rate: Doubling the Sample rate captures the sound wave more accurately but enlarges the file.
Sample resolution: A higher Sample resolution uses more bits to record each audio sample.
Lossless compression: Text files are usually shrunk with Lossless compression so no characters are lost.
Lossy compression: JPEG uses Lossy compression, discarding detail the human eye barely notices.
Primary key: Every student record is uniquely identified by its Primary key.
Foreign key: The OrderTable links to CustomerTable through a Foreign key.
Normalisation: Normalisation removes repeated data so a customer's address is stored only once.
Record: Each Record in the table holds all the data about one student.
Field: The email Field in this table must contain an @ symbol.
Relational database: A Relational database stores customers and orders in separate linked tables.
DBMS: The DBMS controls which users are allowed to update the staff table.
Referential integrity: Referential integrity stops you deleting a customer who still has open orders.
LAN: All the computers in the school library are connected on a single LAN.
WAN: A bank links its branches across the country using a WAN.
Packet: Each Packet carries a header with the destination address and part of the message.
Packet switching: With Packet switching, parts of an email can travel by different routes.
IP address: The router assigns a new IP address to each device that joins the network.
MAC address: A switch forwards a frame using the MAC address burned into each network card.
Bandwidth: Streaming high-definition video needs far more Bandwidth than sending text.
Router: The Router forwards packets between the home network and the internet.
Switch: A Switch sends each frame only to the port of the intended device.
Client-server: In a Client-server network, workstations request files from a central server.
Peer-to-peer: In a Peer-to-peer network, every computer can share files directly with the others.
TCP/IP: The TCP/IP suite breaks a message into packets and reassembles them at the other end.
RAM: When you open more programs, they are all loaded into RAM at once.
ROM: The boot instructions are held in ROM so they survive a power cut.
CPU: The CPU fetches, decodes and executes one instruction after another.
ALU: The ALU performs the addition and comparison operations inside the processor.
Control unit: The Control unit coordinates the fetch-decode-execute cycle across the processor.
Register: The result of the calculation is held briefly in a Register before being stored.
Fetch-decode-execute cycle: The processor repeats the Fetch-decode-execute cycle billions of times a second.
Bus: Data travels between the processor and memory along the Bus.
Volatile: Because RAM is Volatile, unsaved work is lost when the power goes off.
Cache: Frequently used instructions are kept in the Cache to speed up the processor.
Logic gate: An AND gate is a simple Logic gate that outputs 1 only when both inputs are 1.
AND gate: The alarm sounds only when both sensors are triggered, so we use an AND gate.
OR gate: The light turns on if either switch is pressed, which needs an OR gate.
NOT gate: A NOT gate inverts a 1 into a 0 and a 0 into a 1.
NAND gate: A NAND gate outputs 0 only when all of its inputs are 1.
NOR gate: A NOR gate outputs 1 only when every input is 0.
XOR gate: An XOR gate outputs 1 when its two inputs differ from each other.
Truth table: We draw a Truth table to list the output for every possible combination of inputs.
Assembler: An Assembler translates each line of assembly language into one machine-code instruction.
Machine code: The processor can execute Machine code directly without any further translation.
Assembly language: In Assembly language, mnemonics like ADD and LDA stand for machine operations.
High-level language: Python is a High-level language that reads much like ordinary English.
Operating system: The Operating system manages memory and schedules which program runs next.
Utility software: Antivirus scanning is provided by Utility software that maintains the system.
Abstraction: Using Abstraction, a map hides the streets it does not need to show.
Decomposition: Through Decomposition we split the game into input, logic and display modules.
Pseudocode: We plan the algorithm in Pseudocode before writing it in Python.
Linear search: A Linear search checks each item in turn until it finds the target.
Binary search: A Binary search only works if the list has already been sorted.
Bubble sort: A Bubble sort repeatedly swaps adjacent items until the list is in order.
Checksum: The receiver recalculates the Checksum to detect errors during transmission.
Parity bit: An extra Parity bit is added so the number of 1s is always even.
Check digit: The last digit of a barcode is a Check digit used to spot entry errors.
Variable: The Variable count increases by one each time the loop repeats.
Constant: We store pi in a Constant because its value never changes.
Iteration: The loop uses Iteration to process every element of the array.
Selection: An IF statement uses Selection to decide which branch of code runs.
Procedure: A Procedure carries out its task without returning any value.
Function: A Function returns a single value to the line that called it.
Parameter: The subroutine declares a Parameter that receives the value passed in.
Array: We store the ten scores in an Array and access each one by its index.
Sign and magnitude: In Sign and magnitude, the leftmost bit shows whether the number is negative.
Floating-point number: A Floating-point number stores a mantissa and an exponent to cover a wide range.
Mantissa: Normalising a float packs as many significant digits as possible into the Mantissa.
Exponent: Increasing the Exponent shifts the binary point further to the right.
Normalisation (floating-point): Normalisation (floating-point) adjusts the mantissa so the representation is unique.
Character set: Unicode is a Character set large enough to include almost every written script.
Pixel: Each Pixel in the image stores its own colour value.
Metadata: The image file's Metadata records its width, height and colour depth.
Bit rate: The Bit rate equals the sample rate multiplied by the sample resolution.
Subnet mask: The Subnet mask separates the network part of an IP address from the host part.
HTTP: Your browser uses HTTP to request each web page from the server.
HTTPS: The padlock icon shows the site is using HTTPS to encrypt your data.
URL: Typing the URL tells the browser exactly which resource to fetch.
Bit streaming: Bit streaming lets you watch a film while the rest is still downloading.
Latency: Online gamers complain when high Latency delays their actions.
Bus topology: In a Bus topology every device shares one central backbone cable.
Star topology: In a Star topology each computer connects to a central switch.
Wireless access point: Guests connect their phones to the network through the Wireless access point.
Network interface card: Every computer needs a Network interface card to join the network.
Program counter: The Program counter holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched.
Memory address register: The Memory address register holds the address of the memory location being accessed.
Memory data register: Data on its way to or from memory passes through the Memory data register.
Accumulator: The running total of the calculation builds up in the Accumulator.
Data bus: The Data bus carries the actual values between the processor and memory.
Address bus: The Address bus is one-directional and carries the location to be accessed.
Control bus: Timing and command signals are sent along the Control bus.
Clock speed: A higher Clock speed lets the processor complete more cycles each second.
SSD: An SSD has no moving parts, so it reads data faster than a spinning disk.
HDD: An HDD stores data magnetically on rapidly spinning platters.
EEPROM: The BIOS can be updated because it is held in EEPROM, which is rewritable.
Optical storage: A DVD is a form of Optical storage read by a laser detecting pits and lands.
Embedded system: A washing machine is controlled by a small Embedded system.
Non-volatile: A hard disk is Non-volatile, so files remain after the computer is switched off.
Secondary storage: Programs and files are kept permanently in Secondary storage.
Device driver: You must install a Device driver so the operating system can control the printer.
Linker: The Linker joins your compiled modules with library routines into one executable.
Loader: The Loader copies the program from disk into memory ready to run.
Integrated development environment: An Integrated development environment combines an editor, translator and debugger in one tool.
Cloud computing: With Cloud computing, files are stored on remote servers reached over the internet.
Open source software: Linux is Open source software whose code anyone may study and modify.
Proprietary software: Proprietary software keeps its source code secret and restricts copying by licence.
Copyright: Copyright protects the programmer from having their code copied without permission.
Data type: You must choose the correct Data type before declaring the variable.
Boolean: A Boolean flag can hold only the value true or false.
String: We join the first and last names into a single String.
Integer: An Integer variable can only store whole numbers with no decimal part.
Local variable: A Local variable exists only while its subroutine is running.
Global variable: A Global variable can be accessed from anywhere in the program.
Concatenation: Concatenation joins "Hello" and "World" into one longer string.
Nested loop: A Nested loop is used to process every cell of a two-dimensional array.
Serial file: New transactions are simply appended to the end of a Serial file.
Sequential file: Records in a Sequential file are stored in order of their key field.
Trace table: We fill in a Trace table to follow how each variable changes as the loop runs.
Abstraction (OOP): Abstraction (OOP) models a car as a class exposing only its relevant methods.
Class: A Class acts as a template from which many objects can be created.
Object: Each Object is created as an instance of its class with its own attribute values.
Method: We call a Method on the object to change the data it holds.
Attribute: The private Attribute balance is only changed through a setter method.
Constructor: The Constructor runs automatically to initialise a new object's attributes.
Getter: We read the private field through a Getter that returns its value.
Setter: A Setter validates the new value before it changes the private attribute.
Superclass: The Vehicle Superclass passes its shared methods down to Car and Bus.
Subclass: A Subclass inherits the attributes of its parent and can add new ones.
Override: We Override the draw method in the subclass to give it new behaviour.
Abstract data type: A stack is an Abstract data type defined by its operations, not its storage.
Linked list: Each node in a Linked list points to the next node in the sequence.
Binary tree: In a Binary tree each node has at most a left and a right child.
Binary search tree: A Binary search tree keeps smaller values on the left of each node.
Hash table: A Hash table lets us find a record almost instantly from its key.
Hash function: A good Hash function spreads the keys evenly across the table.
Collision: A Collision occurs when two different keys hash to the same index.
Pointer: Each node stores a Pointer holding the address of the next node.
Node: Every Node in the list holds a data item and a link to the next one.
Tuple: A Tuple is ordered but cannot be changed once it has been created.
Record (composite): A Record (composite) groups a name, age and mark under one variable name.
Traversal: An in-order Traversal of a binary search tree outputs the values in sorted order.
Base case: Without a Base case, the recursive call would repeat forever.
Time complexity: The Time complexity of linear search grows in proportion to the list length.
Space complexity: Recursion increases Space complexity because each call adds a stack frame.
Insertion sort: An Insertion sort places each new item into its correct position among the sorted ones.
Merge sort: A Merge sort splits the list, sorts each half and merges them back together.
Divide and conquer: Merge sort uses Divide and conquer by breaking the problem into smaller halves.
Paradigm: Object-oriented programming is a different Paradigm from procedural programming.
Procedural programming: In Procedural programming the solution is written as a series of procedure calls.
Object-oriented programming: Object-oriented programming groups data and methods together inside objects.
Declarative programming: In Declarative programming you state the result you want, not the steps to reach it.
Low-level programming: Low-level programming gives the developer direct control over memory and registers.
DDL: We use DDL commands such as CREATE TABLE to define the database structure.
DML: A SELECT statement is part of DML, which queries and changes the stored data.
SQL: We write an SQL query to retrieve every student born after 2005.
First normal form: A table is in First normal form once it contains no repeating groups.
Second normal form: Second normal form requires every non-key attribute to depend on the whole key.
Third normal form: Third normal form removes attributes that depend on another non-key attribute.
Transaction: A bank Transaction must either complete fully or make no change at all.
Index (database): Adding an Index (database) speeds up searches on the surname column.
Pipelining: Pipelining overlaps the fetch and execute stages of successive instructions.
Interrupt service routine: When a key is pressed, the processor runs the matching Interrupt service routine.
Virtual memory: Virtual memory uses part of the hard disk as an extension of RAM.
Paging: Paging divides memory into fixed-size blocks to manage virtual memory.
Scheduling: Scheduling decides which process gets the processor and for how long.
Symmetric encryption: Symmetric encryption uses the same secret key to lock and unlock the data.
Asymmetric encryption: Asymmetric encryption uses a public key to encrypt and a private key to decrypt.
Digital signature: A Digital signature proves the message came from the sender and was not altered.
SSL/TLS: Websites use SSL/TLS to set up an encrypted connection with your browser.
Firewall: The Firewall blocks incoming traffic that breaks the security rules.
Graph: A Graph models a road network as vertices joined by edges.
Vertex: Each city is drawn as a Vertex in the network graph.
Edge: An Edge connects two vertices and may carry a weight such as distance.
Adjacency matrix: An Adjacency matrix uses a grid to show which vertices are connected.
Adjacency list: An Adjacency list stores, for each vertex, the vertices it links to.
Circular queue: A Circular queue reuses freed space by wrapping the rear back to the start.
Priority queue: In a Priority queue the most urgent item is always removed first.
Dynamic data structure: A linked list is a Dynamic data structure that grows as items are added.
Static data structure: An array is a Static data structure whose size is fixed at compile time.
Chaining: Chaining resolves a collision by storing both items in a list at the same index.
Root node: The Root node sits at the top of the tree and has no parent.
Leaf node: A Leaf node is any node in the tree that has no children.
In-order traversal: An In-order traversal of a binary search tree visits the values in ascending order.
Free list: New nodes are taken from the Free list of currently unused positions.
Constant time: Looking up an array element by its index runs in Constant time.
Linear time: Searching an unsorted list of n items takes Linear time in the worst case.
Quadratic time: A bubble sort on n items runs in Quadratic time as n grows large.
Depth-first search: A Depth-first search explores one branch fully before backtracking.
Breadth-first search: A Breadth-first search visits every neighbour before moving a level deeper.
Stack frame: Each recursive call pushes a new Stack frame holding its own local data.
Call stack: When a subroutine returns, its frame is removed from the Call stack.
Lexical analysis: Lexical analysis groups the source characters into tokens and strips out comments.
Syntax analysis: Syntax analysis checks that the tokens follow the grammar of the language.
Code generation: During Code generation the compiler produces machine code from the parsed program.
Code optimisation: Code optimisation rewrites the output so the finished program runs faster.
Token: The lexer turns the keyword WHILE into a single Token.
Symbol table: The compiler records each variable's type and address in the Symbol table.
Backus-Naur Form: Backus-Naur Form uses rules to define the exact grammar of a language.
Syntax diagram: A Syntax diagram shows the valid structure of a statement as a flow of paths.
Process: The operating system gives each running Process a share of the processor's time.
Thread: A single Thread is one sequence of execution within a process.
Semaphore: A Semaphore signals whether a shared resource is currently free or in use.
Mutual exclusion: Mutual exclusion ensures only one process updates the shared file at a time.
Round robin: A Round robin scheduler gives every process an equal time slice in turn.
Page fault: A Page fault occurs when the needed page is not currently in main memory.
Thrashing: Thrashing wastes time when the processor swaps pages more than it computes.
Kernel: The Kernel is the core of the operating system that manages hardware access.
Garbage collection: Garbage collection automatically frees memory the program no longer references.
Boyce-Codd normal form: Boyce-Codd normal form is stricter than 3NF about determinants being candidate keys.
Atomicity: Atomicity guarantees a transaction is either fully done or not done at all.
Consistency (ACID): Consistency (ACID) means a transaction leaves the database in a valid state.
Isolation: Isolation keeps concurrent transactions from interfering with each other.
Durability: Durability ensures committed changes survive even a later power failure.
Record locking: Record locking stops two users editing the same booking at once.
Candidate key: Either the email or the ID could serve as a Candidate key for the table.
Secondary key: We add a Secondary key so records can also be searched by surname.
Public key: Anyone can encrypt a message using the recipient's Public key.
Private key: Only the owner can decrypt the message with their matching Private key.
Digital certificate: A Digital certificate binds a website's public key to its verified identity.
Hashing: Passwords are stored after Hashing so the originals are never kept in plain text.
Denial of service: A Denial of service attack floods the server so real users cannot connect.
Machine learning: Machine learning lets the system improve at a task by learning from data.
Artificial neural network: An Artificial neural network learns patterns by adjusting the weights between its nodes.
Backpropagation: Backpropagation trains the network by adjusting weights from the output error.
Supervised learning: Supervised learning trains a model on examples already labelled with correct answers.
Unsupervised learning: Unsupervised learning groups similar customers together without any labels.
Binary prefix: The Binary prefix kibi stands for 1024 rather than 1000.
Decimal prefix: The Decimal prefix kilo means one thousand and is a power of ten.
Kibibyte: One Kibibyte is exactly 1024 bytes of memory.
Extended ASCII: Extended ASCII uses eight bits to represent 256 different characters.
Vector graphic: A Vector graphic can be scaled up without becoming blurred.
Drawing object: A circle is one Drawing object within a vector image.
Drawing list: The Drawing list records every shape and its properties in the vector image.
File header: The File header at the start of a bitmap stores its width and colour depth.
Screen resolution: A higher Screen resolution shows more pixels across the display.
Sampling: Sampling measures the sound wave's height at regular time intervals.
Analogue data: A microphone captures Analogue data as a continuously varying voltage.
Digital data: The computer stores Digital data using discrete binary values.
Thin client: A Thin client relies on the server for most of its processing and storage.
Thick client: A Thick client does most of the work locally and depends little on the server.
Mesh topology: A Mesh topology gives redundant paths so data can bypass a broken link.
Hybrid topology: A Hybrid topology combines star and bus layouts within one network.
Ethernet: Ethernet defines how frames are transmitted over a wired local network.
CSMA/CD: Under CSMA/CD a device listens before sending and resends if a collision happens.
Data collision: A Data collision happens when two devices transmit on the shared cable at once.
Bridge: A Bridge connects two network segments and forwards frames between them.
Repeater: A Repeater regenerates a weak signal so it can travel further.
Modem: A Modem converts digital data into an analogue signal for the phone line.
Internet: The Internet is the global network of interconnected computer networks.
IPv4: An IPv4 address is written as four numbers between 0 and 255.
IPv6: An IPv6 address uses 128 bits, giving a far larger pool than IPv4.
Public IP address: A Public IP address is unique across the whole internet and routable globally.
Private IP address: A Private IP address works only inside the local network.
Static IP address: A server is often given a Static IP address that never changes.
Dynamic IP address: A home router usually receives a Dynamic IP address that can change over time.
Fibre-optic cable: A Fibre-optic cable carries data as pulses of light with very high bandwidth.
Copper cable: A Copper cable transmits data as electrical signals along its wires.
Wireless network interface card: A Wireless network interface card lets the laptop join the network using radio signals.
On-demand streaming: On-demand streaming lets viewers start a stored film whenever they like.
Real-time streaming: Real-time streaming sends a live match to viewers as it is being played.
Static RAM: Static RAM needs no refreshing and is fast, so it is used for cache.
Dynamic RAM: Dynamic RAM must be refreshed constantly but is cheaper and denser.
PROM: A PROM can be written once after manufacture and then never changed.
EPROM: An EPROM can be erased with ultraviolet light and then reprogrammed.
Buffer: A print Buffer holds pages temporarily so the CPU need not wait for the printer.
Primary memory: Primary memory, made up of RAM and ROM, is accessed directly by the processor.
Actuator: The controller sends a signal to an Actuator that opens the valve.
Sensor: A temperature Sensor sends readings to the microprocessor for processing.
Feedback: Feedback from the sensor lets the heating system adjust its next action.
Monitoring system: A weather Monitoring system records readings but never controls anything.
Control system: A greenhouse Control system uses sensor data to open the vents automatically.
Core: A single Core can execute one instruction stream on its own.
Bus width: A wider Bus width lets more bits travel in parallel each cycle.
Index register: The Index register holds an offset added to an address during indexed addressing.
Current instruction register: The Current instruction register holds the instruction now being decoded.
Status register: Flags such as carry and zero are stored in the Status register after an operation.
Von Neumann architecture: In Von Neumann architecture, instructions and data share the same memory.
Stored program concept: The Stored program concept keeps instructions in memory alongside the data.
USB: We plug the external drive into a spare USB port on the laptop.
HDMI: An HDMI cable carries both high-definition video and audio to the monitor.
Dynamic link library: Several programs can share the same code held in one Dynamic link library.
Program library: We reuse tested routines from a Program library rather than rewriting them.
Memory management: Memory management allocates and reclaims the memory used by each program.
File management: File management organises files on the disk and controls who may open them.
Process management: Process management schedules processes and shares processor time between them.
Defragmentation: Defragmentation rearranges scattered file fragments into contiguous blocks.
Breakpoint: We set a Breakpoint so the program pauses and we can inspect the variables.
Single stepping: Single stepping runs the program one line at a time to trace a bug.
Prettyprint: The IDE can Prettyprint the code by adding neat indentation and colour.
Malware: Malware is any software written to damage or gain access to a system.
Virus: A Virus attaches to another program and spreads when that program is run.
Spyware: Spyware secretly records what a user types and sends it to an attacker.
Phishing: A Phishing email pretends to be your bank to trick you into revealing a password.
Pharming: Pharming secretly redirects you from a real website to a fake copy.
Biometrics: Unlocking a phone with a fingerprint is a common use of Biometrics.
Authentication: A password provides Authentication that the user is who they claim to be.
Access rights: Access rights decide which files each user is allowed to read or edit.
Entity: In the database each Entity, such as Student, becomes its own table.
Table: Each Table stores rows and columns of data about one type of entity.
Entity-relationship diagram: An Entity-relationship diagram shows how the tables in the design are linked.
One-to-many relationship: One teacher marks many essays, which is a One-to-many relationship.
Many-to-many relationship: Students and clubs form a Many-to-many relationship resolved with a link table.
File-based approach: A File-based approach stores data in separate files, causing needless duplication.
Data redundancy: Data redundancy wastes space by storing the same address in several places.
Sequence: In Sequence, each statement runs one after another in the order written.
Flowchart: We draw a Flowchart with standard symbols to show the steps of the algorithm.
Stepwise refinement: Stepwise refinement breaks the outline into smaller and smaller steps.
Identifier: Each Identifier names a variable, constant or subroutine in the program.
Identifier table: The Identifier table lists every variable used along with its data type.
Range check: A Range check rejects any age that is below 0 or above 120.
Format check: A Format check ensures a date is entered in the pattern DD/MM/YYYY.
Length check: A Length check rejects a password that is shorter than eight characters.
Presence check: A Presence check makes sure the required email field is not left blank.
Parity check: A Parity check counts the 1 bits to detect an error during transmission.
Double entry: Double entry asks you to type the new password twice to catch mistakes.
CASE statement: A CASE statement selects one branch to run based on the menu number entered.
Count-controlled loop: A Count-controlled loop repeats a known, fixed number of times.
Pre-condition loop: A Pre-condition loop tests its condition before the body ever runs.
Post-condition loop: A Post-condition loop always runs its body at least once before testing.
Argument: The value 5 is passed as an Argument to the subroutine when it is called.
Pass by value: With Pass by value, the subroutine works on a copy and cannot change the original.
Pass by reference: With Pass by reference, the subroutine can change the caller's original variable.
Return value: The function's Return value is passed back to the line that called it.
Subroutine: We put the repeated code into a Subroutine so it can be called many times.
Real (data type): A Real (data type) can store a number such as 3.14 with a fractional part.
Char: A Char variable holds a single character such as the letter Q.
Date (data type): A Date (data type) stores a calendar date like 25/12/2025.
Two-dimensional array: A Two-dimensional array stores the grid using a row index and a column index.
Upper bound: The Upper bound is the largest valid index allowed in the array.
Lower bound: The Lower bound is the smallest valid index the array can use.
Totalling: Totalling keeps adding each mark to a running total inside the loop.
Counting: Counting increases a counter by one each time the condition is met.
Text file: We read the report line by line from a Text file.
User-defined data type: A User-defined data type lets the programmer build a new type to fit the problem.
Composite data type: A record is a Composite data type built from several other data items.
Non-composite data type: An enumerated type is a Non-composite data type not built from other types.
Set: A Set stores an unordered collection of values with no duplicates allowed.
Underflow: Underflow occurs when a result is too small to represent in the available bits.
Rounding error: A Rounding error appears because binary can only approximate some decimals.
Random file: Records in a Random file are located directly using a hashing algorithm on the key.
Sequential access: Sequential access reads records one after another from the start of the file.
Direct access: Direct access jumps straight to the required record without reading the earlier ones.
Hashing algorithm: A Hashing algorithm calculates where in the file a record should be stored.
Half adder: A Half adder adds two single bits but has no carry input.
Full adder: A Full adder adds two bits together with a carry-in from the previous column.
Flip-flop: A Flip-flop stores a single bit and changes state when triggered.
SR flip-flop: An SR flip-flop uses Set and Reset inputs to store one bit.
JK flip-flop: A JK flip-flop removes the invalid input state of the SR type and can toggle.
Boolean algebra: We use Boolean algebra to simplify the logic expression before building the circuit.
De Morgan's laws: De Morgan's laws let us turn a NOT of an AND into an OR of two NOTs.
Karnaugh map: A Karnaugh map groups adjacent 1s to simplify a Boolean expression.
RISC: A RISC processor uses a small set of simple instructions that pipeline easily.
CISC: A CISC processor offers many complex instructions, each doing several operations.
SISD: A SISD machine runs one instruction stream on one data stream at a time.
SIMD: A SIMD architecture applies the same instruction to many data items at once.
MIMD: A MIMD system runs different instructions on different data across several processors.
Massively parallel computer: A Massively parallel computer links thousands of processors for high performance.
Virtual machine: A Virtual machine is a software emulation of a computer running on a host.
Parallel processing: Parallel processing speeds up the task by splitting it across several processors.
Multi-tasking: Multi-tasking switches quickly between programs so they seem to run at once.
Running state: A process in the Running state currently holds and is using the processor.
Ready state: A process in the Ready state is waiting only to be given the processor.
Blocked state: A process enters the Blocked state while it waits for input to arrive.
Shortest job first: Shortest job first runs the process with the smallest estimated time next.
First come first served: First come first served gives the processor to processes in their order of arrival.
Shortest remaining time: Shortest remaining time preempts the current process if a shorter one arrives.
Segmentation: Segmentation divides memory into variable-length parts matching the program's logic.
Interrupt handler: The Interrupt handler in the kernel responds to and services each interrupt.
Multi-core processor: A Multi-core processor runs several instruction streams at the same time.
Plaintext: The Plaintext is the readable message before it is encrypted.
Ciphertext: Encrypting the message turns the plaintext into unreadable Ciphertext.
Symmetric key cryptography: Symmetric key cryptography uses one shared secret key to encrypt and decrypt.
Asymmetric key cryptography: Asymmetric key cryptography pairs a public key with a matching private key.
Quantum cryptography: Quantum cryptography can detect an eavesdropper trying to intercept the key.
Certificate authority: A Certificate authority is the trusted party that issues digital certificates.
A* algorithm: The A* algorithm combines the cost so far with a heuristic to find a shortest path.
Dijkstra's algorithm: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from one vertex to all the others.
Heuristic: A Heuristic gives a quick estimate that guides the search toward the goal.
Deep learning: Deep learning uses neural networks with many layers to learn complex patterns.
Reinforcement learning: In Reinforcement learning an agent learns from rewards and penalties by trial and error.
Regression: Regression fits a line to the data to predict a continuous numeric value.
Weight (neural network): A Weight (neural network) scales an input and is adjusted during training.
Dictionary: A Dictionary stores key-value pairs so a value can be looked up by its key.
Unwinding: Unwinding returns through the recursive calls once the base case is reached.
Imperative programming: Imperative programming lists explicit statements that change the program's state.
Aggregation: Aggregation lets a Team object hold references to several Player objects.
Instance: Each Instance is a specific object created from its class.
Fact: In Prolog a Fact states a piece of information that is taken to be true.
Rule: In declarative programming a Rule derives new conclusions from known facts.
Exception: An Exception such as dividing by zero interrupts the normal flow of the program.
